b: An AI-native OS-layer is won by a superior conversational/agentic UI.
a: UI is a replaceable SURFACE (render + input). What makes a thing an OS is the SUBSTRATE it owns — brokerage, persistent governed memory, identity, capability, sovereignty. An AI can regenerate any UI overnight; it cannot cheaply regenerate the substrate or the trust/identity graph bound to it. Survivability lives in the substrate, not the surface.
tg: T6: across 7 super-apps the UI was freely skinned/cloned; survivors owned OS-type primitives (runtime, payments-as-infra, identity, governance).

b: OS-type features are a flat checklist you either have or don't.
a: They form a SURVIVABILITY LADDER (binned). Bin0 UI/surface (render, theming, view injection, widgets) → Bin1 State/Memory (append-only tape, local-first store, cross-session persistence) → Bin2 Brokerage (single signal authority: envelope stamping, capability enforcement, fan-out) → Bin3 Identity/Capability (tenant/user, least-privilege gate) → Bin4 Governance/Sovereignty (compliance-mode gate, statutory ledger, residency boundary, intent-authorization) → Bin5 Economic rail (payments-as-infra, transaction tax, cross-service optimization). Durability RISES as you descend the ladder.
tg: Synthesis of T6 mechanisms against the Canvas codebase primitives (broker/gate/tape/identity).

b: Governance is compliance overhead — bolt it on after product-market fit.
a: Governance (Bin4) is the SURVIVAL FLOOR AND CEILING. Every super-app died at the governance wall, not the tech wall: Ant Group 313e9 USD IPO to 0, LINE 510K-breach to forced parent divestiture, Kakao 40M-user sharing ruled illegal. It is the highest-survivability bin because it is simultaneously legally mandatory AND the hardest thing to retrofit at scale. Owning it natively is the durable moat; lacking it is fatal.
tg: T6 governance-crisis column + Canada legal layer P0 directives (compliance-mode router, statutory ledger).

b: If it has a rich UI and calls some backend APIs, it's an OS-layer.
a: The KERNEL TEST is brokerage: is there a SINGLE authority every signal must pass through? An app calls APIs; an OS brokers them. If any signal can bypass the broker (the one authority for envelope stamping, capability enforcement, fan-out, telemetry tap), it is a UI with extra steps, not an OS. The chokepoint is the OS.
tg: Canvas broker (apps/mac/CanvasBroker/Broker.swift) already implements this; it is the structural test for OS-ness.

b: State is a per-session implementation detail of the app.
a: Persistent, governed MEMORY across sessions and apps (Bin1: the append-only tape + local-first store) is what makes the layer 'breathing' — it survives the app being closed and arbitrates state the user never re-enters. A UI forgets; an OS remembers and governs what is remembered. The tape is both memory and the audit substrate governance (Bin4) is built on.
tg: T6 unified-cloud/identity mechanism (LINE Flava) + Canvas tape (canvas.governance_entries).

b: Payments are a feature to add once we have users.
a: The economic rail (Bin5: payments-as-infrastructure, transaction tax, cross-service optimization) is the DEEPEST moat — WeChat Pay/Alipay processed 256K TPS, ~4x Visa, making payment infrastructure not a feature — but it is also the HIGHEST-RISK bin: embedded UNREGULATED finance is annihilation (Ant). Highest survivability only IF structurally separated/licensed (RPAA/AML); otherwise it is the kill-switch regulators pull.
tg: T6 payment-rail column + Canada RPAA/PCMLTFA failure points; lesson regca-l-03 (payments are reg-core from day one).

b: Our defensibility is the quality and polish of the experience.
a: THE CLONE TEST for survivability: what survives if a competitor copies your UI overnight? If copying the UI kills you, you were a UI. If your identity graph, governed memory, and brokered capabilities make the clone a hollow shell with no users' data, trust, or compliance posture, you were an OS. Design so the answer is the latter — the moat is what the clone cannot copy: the substrate and the trust bound to it.
tg: T6: mini-program UIs were cloned freely; the runtime/identity/governance substrate was not.

b: Build the most visible/impressive features first.
a: BINNING RULE: tag every feature by the lowest survivability bin it serves, then invest scarce build DOWN the ladder. Bin0 (UI) is necessary but confers ~zero durability — an AI clones it. Allocate disproportionate effort to Bin2-4 (brokerage, identity, governance) because those compound into survivability and are precisely what incumbents bolt on too late. Visibility is not durability.
tg: Prioritization heuristic derived from L1-L7; directly shapes the Canvas build sequence (governance engine before inference router).

q: Can the per-bin survivability contribution be quantified (e.g. churn/displacement resistance per bin) rather than ranked ordinally, so the binning rule has weights not just an ordering?
w: Without weights, 'invest down-ladder' is directional; weights would let us trade a Bin2 feature against two Bin0 features with evidence.
